name: Madrigueras and Júcar Gorge Loop
name_original: Valdeganga, Motilleja y Modrigueras — circular desde Jorquera
description: This moderate 50.4-mile road cycling route from Jorquera explores the scenic Júcar River valley, passing through Valdeganga, Motilleja, and
region_intro: Road cycling around Jorquera features routes that traverse the varied terrain of the Júcar river basin. The region is characterized by the dramatic Júcar Gorge, offering scenic views and undulating roads. Cyclists can expect a mix of open landscapes, agricultural areas, and sections that follow the river's course. The elevation changes provide both challenging climbs and flatter stretches suitable for different skill levels. Best road cycling routes around Jorquera The most popular road cycling route is Madrigueras and Júcar Gorge Loop, a 50.4 miles (81.1 km) trail that takes 3 hours 25 minutes to complete. This route offers views of the Júcar Gorge and connects to Madrigueras. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Cuco of Valdeganga – Chinchilla de Monte-Aragón loop from Valdeganga, a difficult 51.1 miles (82.2 km) path. This route explores the areas around Valdeganga and Chinchilla de Monte-Aragón, presenting varied landscapes. Local road cyclists also love the View of the Júcar – Alcalá del Júcar loop from Jorquera, a 42.2 miles (67.9 km) trail leading through the Júcar river valley, often completed in about 3 hours 7 minutes. Road cycling around Jorquera is defined by river gorges, open plains, and rolling hills. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 200 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Jorquera's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling around Jorquera?
A: Road cycling around Jorquera features routes that traverse the varied terrain of the Júcar river basin. The region is characterized by the dramatic Júcar Gorge, offering scenic views and undulating roads. Cyclists can expect a mix of open landscapes, agricultural areas, and sections that follow the river's course. The elevation changes provide both challenging climbs and flatter stretches suitable for different skill levels.

Q: What are the difficulty levels for road cycling routes in Jorquera?
A: Jorquera offers a diverse range of road cycling routes suitable for various skill levels. You'll find options for easy, moderate, and difficult rides. There are 6 easy routes, 16 moderate routes, and 3 difficult routes available, ensuring something for every cyclist.

Q: Are there any road cycling routes that are particularly scenic or offer great views?
A: Absolutely. Many routes in Jorquera highlight the region's natural beauty. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20130145" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Júcar – Alcalá del Júcar loop from Jorquera&lt;/a&gt; takes you through the stunning Júcar river valley. The area is known for its dramatic gorges, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2595067" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Júcar River Gorge Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which offers spectacular vistas.

Q: What is the typical length and duration of road cycling routes in Jorquera?
A: The road cycling routes around Jorquera vary significantly in length and duration. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1153492824/madrigueras-and-jucar-gorge-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Madrigueras and Júcar Gorge Loop&lt;/a&gt; is about 50.4 miles (81.1 km) and typically takes around 3 hours 25 minutes. Other routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20129994" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Santa Quiteria – Higueruela loop from Casas de Juan Núñez&lt;/a&gt;, are shorter at 29.8 miles (47.9 km) and can be completed in about 2 hours 9 minutes.

Q: Are there any loop routes for road cycling around Jorquera?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes in Jorquera are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Popular options include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1153492824/madrigueras-and-jucar-gorge-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Madrigueras and Júcar Gorge Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38159686" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuco of Valdeganga – Chinchilla de Monte-Aragón loop from Valdeganga&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are some interesting places to explore or landmarks to see near the road cycling routes?
A: Beyond the cycling, the region offers several fascinating attractions. You can visit the historic town of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/184407" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alcalá del Júcar&lt;/a&gt;, explore the unique &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4787362" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Devil's Caves&lt;/a&gt;, or enjoy the natural beauty of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1274815" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jorquera River Pool&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes pass close to these points of interest.

Q: What is the best time of year for road biking in Jorquera?
A: While specific weather conditions can vary, the region's climate generally makes spring and autumn ideal for road cycling. Temperatures are milder, and the landscapes are particularly vibrant. Summer can be quite hot, especially in the middle of the day, so early morning or late afternoon rides are recommended during those months.

Q: Are there any beginner-friendly road cycling routes in Jorquera?
A: Yes, Jorquera offers several easy-grade road cycling routes perfect for beginners or those looking for a more relaxed ride. These routes typically feature less elevation gain and smoother terrain, allowing for an enjoyable introduction to road cycling in the area.

Q: Where can I find refreshments or cafes along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes pass through or near small towns and villages where you can find cafes, bars, or small shops to refuel. Planning your route with komoot allows you to see points of interest and potential stops along the way, ensuring you can find refreshments when needed.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Jorquera?
A: The komoot community frequently praises the diverse landscapes and the dramatic beauty of the Júcar Gorge. Cyclists often highlight the mix of challenging climbs and scenic river-following sections. The variety of routes, from moderate to difficult, also receives positive feedback, catering to different fitness levels.

Q: Are there any advanced or challenging road cycling routes in Jorquera?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, Jorquera offers several difficult-grade routes. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38159686" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuco of Valdeganga – Chinchilla de Monte-Aragón loop from Valdeganga&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 51.1 miles (82.2 km) with significant elevation changes, providing a demanding and rewarding experience.
tips: Jorquera is one of the most impressive municipalities in Castilla-La Mancha. Located on a rocky hill surrounded by the Júcar river. Here, just below, next to the dam on the river, some natural pools were formed, a mandatory stop to rest in the summer. Swimming area with natural shade and a place to have a picnic.
Asphalted path through the Júcar river gorge. We can observe the impressive rocky walls. Magnificent views.

Municipality located on the right bank of the Júcar river. In the Plaza del Ayuntamiento there are some bars if you need to recharge your batteries. On Calle Valencia you can see an old “cuco” (egg-shaped building made of stone and clay).
Place where the N-322 road crosses the river Júcar. Parallel to the road there is an old bridge if you want to stop - on the national road there can be quite a bit of traffic.
Beautiful baroque church dedicated to Saints San Pedro and San Pablo in the center of Madrigueras. Right next to it is the curious building of the town hall.
View of an old mill located on the outskirts of the village of Mahora. You can get closer but by a dirt road.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: This circular route is approximately 81 kilometers long and is rated as moderate. For a racebike, it typically takes around 3 hours and 25 minutes of active cycling time to complete, depending on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is classified as a moderate road ride, featuring mostly well-paved surfaces. However, be aware that some segments may be unpaved and more challenging to ride, requiring good fitness and potentially a suitable bike setup. The elevation gain is around 600 meters, meaning you'll encounter notable ascents and descents as you navigate the Júcar River valley.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty rating, 81 km distance, and over 600 meters of elevation gain, this route is best suited for cyclists with good fitness and some experience. Beginners might find the distance and varied terrain, including potentially unpaved sections, quite challenging.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Jorquera. You can typically find parking within the town of Jorquera, which serves as a convenient starting point for this circular tour. Look for public parking areas or street parking that complies with local regulations.

faqs.question: What are the main sights and natural features along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll experience picturesque views of the Júcar River valley, including gorges and canyons. Notable highlights include the Natural pools at the Júcar Dam, the scenic Júcar River Gorge Trail, and the Júcar River itself. You'll also pass through charming towns like Valdeganga, where you can see the Cuco of Valdeganga, and Madrigueras, which features a religious building.

faqs.question: Are there any scenic viewpoints on the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route offers continuous scenic beauty, particularly along the Júcar River. You can expect wonderful views of the Rio Júcar valley, especially between Valdeganga and Alcalá del Júcar. Keep an eye out for the View of the Mahora windmill, which provides a great photo opportunity.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during spring and autumn when temperatures are milder and the landscape is vibrant. Summer can be very hot in this region, making cycling less comfortable, while winter might bring colder temperatures and potentially less predictable weather.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for cycling in the Hoz del Júcar area?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads and established routes like this one in the Hoz del Júcar area. However, always respect local regulations, especially if you venture into protected natural spaces or private land.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally welcome in many outdoor areas, this route is primarily a road cycling tour. Bringing a dog along for an 81 km racebike ride might not be practical or safe for the animal, especially given the potential for traffic and varied surfaces. If you plan to bring a dog, consider shorter, dedicated walking or hiking trails in the area instead.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling or hiking trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers sections of the Sendero del Rio Jucar and the Cumbres y barrancos Hoz del Júcar. You may also briefly encounter parts of Las ramblas de la Abadia y el Batán and Ruta Destroyer.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or find refreshments along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through several towns including Jorquera, Valdeganga, Motilleja, and Madrigueras, you will find opportunities for refreshments. These towns typically have local cafes, restaurants, or small shops where you can refuel and take a break.

waypoints.name: Natural pools at the Júcar Dam
